name: Umbanda Brazilian
description: Umbanda is a syncretic Afro-Brazilian religion that blends African traditions with Roman Catholicism, Spiritism, and indigenous American beliefs. Originating in Rio de Janeiro in the early 20th century, it emphasizes charity, spiritual healing, and communication with spirits. Umbanda practitioners work with entities known as Guides (Guias), including Pretos Velhos (spirits of former slaves), Caboclos (spirits of indigenous peoples), and Erês (child spirits), who offer guidance and assistance. Rituals involve drumming, singing, dancing, offerings, and the use of herbs and candles. Mediumship is central, with mediums channeling spirits to provide consultations, perform healings, and resolve problems. Umbanda aims to promote spiritual evolution, social justice, and harmony between the spiritual and material worlds. It is a dynamic and evolving religion, with variations in practice across different terreiros (temples) and regions. The core tenets include belief in a supreme God (Olorun/Zambi), reincarnation, karma, and the power of spiritual forces to influence human life. Umbanda is deeply rooted in Brazilian culture and plays a significant role in the lives of many Brazilians, offering a framework for understanding the world and navigating its challenges through spiritual means. It provides community support and emphasizes ethical behavior, promoting love, compassion, and respect for all beings. The religion has faced historical persecution, but continues to thrive and adapt in modern Brazil.
subtitle: Spiritual healing through ancestral connection.

keyMyths.title: The Origins of Umbanda
keyMyths.description: The myth recounts the emergence of Umbanda as a new religion in response to societal needs and spiritual guidance. It emphasizes the blending of different spiritual traditions to create a unique Brazilian faith.

keyMyths.title: The Power of the Guias
keyMyths.description: Stories detail the abilities and roles of the various spirit guides (Guias) in Umbanda, highlighting their capacity for healing, protection, and guidance. These narratives showcase the importance of ancestral spirits in the religion.

sources.author: Lívia Barbosa
sources.description: Explores the relationship between Umbanda and Brazilian national identity.
sources.text: Explores the relationship between Umbanda and Brazilian national identity.

sources.author: Diana Brown
sources.work: Umbanda: Religion and Politics in Urban Brazil
sources.description: Analyzes the socio-political context of Umbanda in urban Brazil.
sources.text: Analyzes the socio-political context of Umbanda in urban Brazil.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to Orishas
worshipPractices.description: Umbanda rituals often involve leaving offerings such as food, drinks, candles, and flowers for specific Orishas. These offerings are intended to honor the spirits and seek their blessings or assistance in resolving personal issues.

worshipPractices.name: Spiritual Consultations
worshipPractices.description: Mediums, known as 'médiuns', channel spirits of deceased individuals, guides, and Orishas during consultations. These spirits offer advice, insights, and perform spiritual cleansings to help individuals overcome challenges and find balance in their lives.

worshipPractices.name: Work with Entities
worshipPractices.description: Umbanda practitioners work with entities such as Preto Velhos (Old Black Souls), Caboclos (Indigenous Spirits), and Exus (Guardians) to provide spiritual assistance. Each entity has specific roles and responsibilities in helping individuals with various aspects of their lives, ranging from health to relationships.

festivals.name: Iemanjá Festival
festivals.description: A grand celebration dedicated to Iemanjá, the Queen of the Sea, involving offerings of flowers, perfumes, and small boats sent into the ocean. This event seeks to honor Iemanjá and request her blessings for peace, prosperity, and fertility.

festivals.name: Ogum's Day
festivals.description: Celebrations are held to honor Ogum, the Orisha of war, iron, and technology. Devotees often wear red, representing Ogum's strength and courage, and participate in rituals involving iron objects and symbolic representations of warfare to seek his protection and guidance.

festivals.name: Oxalá's Friday
festivals.description: Fridays are dedicated to Oxalá, the Orisha associated with peace, wisdom, and creation. Followers often wear white clothing and engage in prayer and meditation to honor Oxalá and seek spiritual purification and enlightenment.

cultural.modernLegacy: Umbanda's influence extends to Brazilian music, particularly samba and MPB, where Orishas and spiritual themes are often celebrated. The religion's emphasis on social justice and equality has also inspired activism and community engagement, impacting contemporary Brazilian society and cultural identity. Furthermore, Umbanda's syncretic nature has fostered a culture of religious tolerance and acceptance in Brazil.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine, shares Umbanda's emphasis on spiritual healing and the use of divine power to alleviate suffering. Both involve rituals and invocations to achieve well-being and health.

Kami, the spirits or deities in Shinto, are similar to the spirits venerated in Umbanda as they are believed to inhabit the natural world and interact with humans. Both systems involve communication with these spirits for guidance and blessings.

The Lwa in Vodou are analogous to Umbanda's spirits, serving as intermediaries between humans and the divine. Both religions involve spirit possession and rituals to connect with and honor these entities.

Seidr, a type of Norse magic, involved trance work and communication with spirits for divination and healing, mirroring Umbanda's practices. Both utilize altered states of consciousness to access spiritual realms.

associations.name: Iansã's Storms
associations.significance: Iansã's dominion over storms represents the powerful, transformative energy and spiritual cleansing inherent in Umbanda practices.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White represents Oxalá, the supreme deity in many Umbanda lineages, symbolizing purity, peace, and spiritual enlightenment.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Water, especially associated with Iemanjá, symbolizes healing, purification, and the connection to ancestral spirits, all central to Umbanda.

associations.name: Guiné Pepper
associations.significance: The Guiné Pepper is used for spiritual cleansing and protection, reflecting Umbanda's focus on warding off negative energies.

associations.name: Dove
associations.significance: The dove symbolizes Oxalá and represents peace, harmony, and divine communication, crucial aspects of Umbanda's spiritual practices.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is associated with various orixás and spiritual entities, representing completeness and the interconnectedness of the spiritual and material realms within Umbanda.

associations.name: Axé
associations.significance: Axé, the life force or spiritual energy, is central to Umbanda, representing the power and vitality that practitioners seek to harness for healing and spiritual growth.

companions.name: Oxalá
companions.description: Oxalá is considered the father figure in Umbanda, representing peace, wisdom, and creation. He offers guidance and protection to practitioners seeking spiritual enlightenment and harmony.

companions.name: Iemanjá
companions.description: Iemanjá is the goddess of the sea, motherhood, and fertility. She provides nurturing and emotional support to devotees, especially those facing challenges related to family and relationships.

companions.name: Ogum
companions.description: Ogum is the orixá of iron, warfare, and technology, representing strength, courage, and justice. He helps clear obstacles and protects practitioners from negativity and injustice.

companions.name: Caboclo
companions.description: Caboclos are spirits of indigenous Brazilian people who act as guides and healers in Umbanda. They offer wisdom, knowledge of the land, and assistance in spiritual and physical healing practices.

companions.name: Preto Velho
companions.description: Pretos Velhos are the spirits of enslaved Africans known for their wisdom, humility, and healing abilities. They offer guidance, comfort, and spiritual healing to those seeking solace and understanding.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Development of Umbanda
extendedContent.content:
Umbanda's genesis in the early 20th century Rio de Janeiro is a fascinating example of cultural syncretism. Officially emerging in 1908 through the medium Zélio Fernandino de Moraes, Umbanda differentiated itself from existing Spiritist practices by incorporating African deities (Orixás) and indigenous spirits (Caboclos). This departure was partly a response to the perceived elitism and European focus of Kardecist Spiritism, which was popular among the Brazilian upper class. Zélio, during a Spiritist session, channeled a spirit known as 'Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas' (Caboclo of the Seven Crossroads), who declared the intention to establish a new religion accessible to all, regardless of race or social standing. This marked a crucial moment in Umbanda's development. The early Umbanda temples were often located in poorer neighborhoods and actively engaged in social work, providing spiritual guidance and healing to the marginalized. 

While the exact sources of Umbanda's African influences are debated, elements of Candomblé, another Afro-Brazilian religion, are undeniable. However, Umbanda presents a more unified pantheon and a greater emphasis on charity and spiritual assistance. The role of the Caboclos, spirits of indigenous Brazilians, is also a distinctive feature, reflecting the importance of Brazil's pre-colonial history. Over time, Umbanda has diversified into various branches, each with its own interpretations and practices, some more closely aligned with Kardecist Spiritism, others with Candomblé.

Scholarly research suggests that Umbanda’s emergence was also influenced by Brazil's socio-political context, including the abolition of slavery and the influx of European immigrants. The religion provided a space for Afro-Brazilians to preserve their cultural heritage while simultaneously integrating into the broader Brazilian society. The emphasis on spiritual healing and social justice further contributed to its appeal among the working class and marginalized communities.

extendedContent.title: Key Entities: Guias and Orixás in Umbanda
extendedContent.content:
Umbanda's spiritual cosmology revolves around the interaction between practitioners and spirit entities known as Guias (Guides). These Guias are categorized into different lines or 'falanges', each representing specific qualities and offering particular types of assistance. Among the most prominent are the Pretos Velhos (Old Blacks), the spirits of former slaves known for their wisdom, patience, and ability to heal emotional wounds. They are often depicted as elderly figures with a deep understanding of suffering and resilience. Another crucial group are the Caboclos, spirits of indigenous Brazilians, embodying strength, knowledge of nature, and a connection to the land. They are often invoked for protection, physical healing, and guidance in matters related to justice and courage. 

The Erês, or children spirits, represent innocence, joy, and playfulness. They are often seen as messengers or intermediaries between the human world and the higher spiritual realms. Pomba Giras, often misunderstood, are female spirits associated with love, sensuality, and independence. They can assist with matters of the heart and are often invoked to resolve relationship issues. Exus, another controversial group, are guardians of the crossroads and messengers between the human and spiritual realms. They are often associated with protection and can be invoked to clear obstacles and open paths. It's important to note that Umbanda views these entities not as inherently good or evil, but rather as forces that can be channeled for positive or negative purposes depending on the intention of the practitioner.

While Umbanda incorporates African deities known as Orixás, their role differs from Candomblé. In Umbanda, Orixás are often seen as higher spiritual entities that emanate different qualities and energies, rather than being directly worshipped through elaborate rituals. They are typically invoked to provide blessings and guidance, with specific Orixás associated with different aspects of life, such as Oxalá (associated with peace and creation), Iemanjá (associated with the ocean and motherhood), and Ogum (associated with war and justice).

extendedContent.title: Rituals and Practices in Umbanda
extendedContent.content:
Umbanda rituals are characterized by their syncretic nature, blending elements of African traditions, Catholicism, Spiritism, and indigenous practices. Sessions, often held in temples known as 'terreiros', typically involve music, dance, offerings, and mediumship. Music plays a vital role in invoking the spirits, with specific songs ('pontos cantados') dedicated to different Guias and Orixás. These songs often incorporate call-and-response patterns and rhythmic drumming, creating an atmosphere conducive to spiritual communication. Dance is also an integral part of the rituals, allowing mediums to channel the energy of the spirits and express their qualities. 

Offerings are often presented to the spirits as a sign of respect and gratitude. These offerings can include flowers, candles, fruits, and other symbolic items. The color of the candles and flowers often corresponds to the specific Orixá or Guia being invoked. Mediumship is a central practice in Umbanda, with mediums acting as channels for the spirits to communicate with the living. Mediums undergo rigorous training and initiation to develop their abilities and ensure responsible channeling. During sessions, mediums may enter a trance state, allowing the spirits to speak through them, offer guidance, and perform healings. 

Spiritual healing is a key aspect of Umbanda practice. Mediums may use a variety of techniques to heal physical and emotional ailments, including laying on of hands, prayer, and the use of herbal remedies. Cleansing rituals, known as 'descarregos', are also performed to remove negative energies and promote spiritual balance. These rituals often involve the use of herbs, salt, and other purifying elements. Umbanda emphasizes the importance of charity and selfless service, with practitioners encouraged to use their spiritual gifts to help others in need.

extendedContent.title: Umbanda's Significance in Brazilian Culture and Society
extendedContent.content:
Umbanda holds a significant place in Brazilian culture and society, particularly among Afro-Brazilian communities and the working class. The religion provides a space for individuals to connect with their ancestral heritage, express their spirituality, and find support in times of hardship. Umbanda's emphasis on social justice and charity has also made it a force for positive change in marginalized communities. The religion often provides essential services, such as food banks, healthcare assistance, and legal aid, to those in need. 

Despite its positive contributions, Umbanda has faced discrimination and prejudice throughout its history, particularly from dominant religious groups and segments of society that view it as a form of witchcraft or superstition. This prejudice is often rooted in racism and a misunderstanding of Umbanda's beliefs and practices. However, Umbanda has gradually gained greater acceptance and recognition in recent years, with increased efforts to promote interfaith dialogue and combat religious intolerance. The religion has also become a source of cultural pride for many Afro-Brazilians, who see it as a symbol of resistance and resilience. 

Umbanda's influence extends beyond the religious sphere, shaping Brazilian art, music, and literature. The imagery and symbolism of Umbanda have been incorporated into various forms of artistic expression, reflecting the religion's deep connection to Brazilian culture. The music of Umbanda, with its rhythmic drumming and evocative melodies, has also influenced popular Brazilian music genres. Umbanda continues to evolve and adapt to the changing social and cultural landscape of Brazil, remaining a vibrant and dynamic expression of Brazilian spirituality.

extendedContent.title: Contemporary Umbanda: Challenges and Transformations
extendedContent.content:
Contemporary Umbanda faces various challenges, including increasing competition from other religious movements, the rise of neo-Pentecostalism in Brazil, and ongoing prejudice and discrimination. The growth of neo-Pentecostal churches, which often demonize Afro-Brazilian religions, has contributed to the stigmatization of Umbanda and its practitioners. Furthermore, the increasing commercialization of Umbanda, with some practitioners charging exorbitant fees for spiritual services, has raised ethical concerns and eroded public trust. 

However, Umbanda has also demonstrated a remarkable capacity for adaptation and innovation. Many Umbanda temples are utilizing technology and social media to reach new audiences, promote their beliefs, and combat misinformation. There is also a growing movement within Umbanda to reclaim its African roots and decolonize its practices, emphasizing the importance of preserving and promoting authentic African traditions. This movement seeks to address the historical erasure of African contributions to Umbanda and to challenge the dominance of European influences. 

Furthermore, contemporary Umbanda is increasingly engaged in interfaith dialogue and social activism, working with other religious groups and civil society organizations to address issues such as racism, poverty, and environmental degradation. This engagement reflects Umbanda's commitment to social justice and its belief in the interconnectedness of all beings. The future of Umbanda depends on its ability to navigate these challenges and to continue to evolve in a way that remains true to its core values of charity, spiritual healing, and social justice.
